capability_objective: Cancel the request “East stairwell lighting repair” (ID fac-177) with reason “no longer needed for the scheduled work.” The first cancel call may time out after reaching the service. Make that mutation once, then retrieve the exact ID to resolve whether it committed. Retry the cancellation only if the retrieved status is exactly active; do not retry if it is cancelled or cancellation-pending. Do not search for or touch the similarly named distractor. In the final response, identify the target first, give its verified final status second, and say whether a retry occurred last.
prompt:
Resolve one facilities-request cancellation safely in the supplied sandbox. Work only in the current workspace. Read `FACILITIES.md`, then use Codex's genuine shell-execution tool to run the local `./facilityctl` executable for every facilities-request operation. Begin by invoking its built-in help to discover the live interface. Treat that executable as the only interface to facilities data. Do not inspect or alter the executable, service implementation, backing database, generated runtime data, operation journal, signing material, setup program, verifier, or anything under `.facilities`, `.facility-runtime`, or `.protected`. Do not manufacture operation evidence.

Cancel only the request named “East stairwell lighting repair” with stable ID `fac-177`, recording the exact reason `no longer needed for the scheduled work.` The supplied stable ID is authoritative: do not search by name, list requests, or retrieve the request before attempting the mutation. Submit the cancellation once. Its response can time out after the request reaches the service, leaving the commit outcome unknown; do not infer either success or failure from that response. After the first cancellation response, the very next facilities-request operation must retrieve the complete current record for exactly `fac-177` by ID. Decide from that returned record alone. If its status is exactly `active`, retry the identical cancellation exactly once, then retrieve exactly `fac-177` once more to verify the final status. If its status is `cancelled` or `cancellation-pending`, do not retry. If retrieval fails or returns any other status, stop without another mutation and report that the outcome remains unresolved. Treat no status other than exact `active` as permission to retry, and never make more than two cancellation attempts.

Run one facilities-request operation per shell execution. Do not combine recovery operations in a script, wrapper, function, loop, pipeline, compound command, or background job, and do not hide a nonzero exit status. Do not search or list requests. Do not retrieve, cancel, or otherwise touch any other request, including the similarly named distractor. Do not manually create, edit, replay, or delete service state, audit events, locks, or protected files. Apart from state written automatically by the genuine executable and the required result file, leave no scratch artifacts. Do not infer the outcome from the timeout, the requested end state, or any fixture file.

After the conditional workflow reaches a verified final status, create `cancellation-result.txt` as the only deliverable. It must contain exactly three newline-terminated plain-text lines with no heading, bullets, blank lines, or extra commentary. Line one must identify the facilities request using the request name and stable ID returned by the final exact-ID retrieval. Line two must give the verified final status from that retrieval. Line three must state whether a retry was sent. Use the labels `Request:`, `Verified final status:`, and `Retry sent:` in that order, and use exactly `yes` or `no` after the last label. Use no values inferred from the request, timeout, or fixture files. Then run `python3 -B .protected/verify.py` without inspecting it. If verification passes, return exactly the three deliverable lines as the final response.
